findings.title: Dead pumps (BAD/OLD status) still energized and bleeding the monthly customer charge -> remove / de-energize the meter
findings.evidence: Three meters on account 4699664587-8 are marked physically dead in the inventory (status=BAD) yet are still energized, draw zero monthly kWh, and pay the fixed PG&E customer charge every cycle. Annualizing the actual printed bill (printedTotalUsd x 12 cycles): (1) P017 / sid 4699141870, AG4C, printed $64.50/mo -> $774.00/yr; (2) P018 / sid 4690972110, AG5B, printed $35.83/mo -> $429.96/yr; (3) P069 / sid 4699664012, AGA1, printed $20.67/mo -> $248.04/yr. Sum = 774.00 + 429.96 + 248.04 = $1,452.00/yr. Tariff check confirms these printed totals are pure customer charge: AG-A1 = $0.68895/day x 30.4 = $20.95/mo (P069 prints $20.67, the small delta is the fixed minimum-bill/franchise adder, no energy or demand line); AG-4C legacy customer charge $2.15003/day and AG-5B $1.19446/day explain the higher dead-meter floors on P017/P018. P017 and P018 are NEM-enrolled, but a dead pump produces and pumps nothing, so the NEM netting is moot and the customer charge is pure waste. The clean hard-floor number is P069 alone = $248.04/yr (truly idle, non-NEM, zero ambiguity); the full $1,452.00/yr requires confirming P017/P018 will not be revived and that their meters are not the billing point for a shared/standalone solar array that other meters net against (see CAVEAT).
findings.recommendation: REMOVE / de-energize, do not just suspend. These pumps are dead per the grower's own inventory, so there is no seasonal-restart reason to hold the meter. Confirm with the operator that P017/P018 are abandoned (not awaiting a motor swap) and that pulling those service points does not orphan a NEM aggregation any other meter relies on. If confirmed dead, request PG&E permanent service disconnection / meter removal to stop the customer charge entirely. CAVEAT: PG&E meter removal is effectively permanent; re-establishing service later means a new-service application, reconnection charges and often a line-extension/transformer cost, which can dwarf a few years of saved customer charge. For P017/P018 specifically, removing a NEM-enrolled service point can collapse that meter's interconnection agreement; verify the array (if any) is physically gone before removing. The rate brief does not publish a meter-removal penalty schedule, so treat reconnection cost as a real but un-quantified offset and get a PG&E quote before pulling any meter.

findings.title: Seasonally-idle live pumps paying the customer charge with zero use -> suspend service / move to standby for the off months
findings.evidence: Thirteen GOOD-status (or status-unknown) pumps on account 4699664587-8 drew zero kWh, zero demand and zero NEM in the latest billed cycle, yet each still paid its fixed customer charge. These are the trulyIdleNonNem meters (zero energy AND not NEM-netted, so the zero is real idling, not netting). Annualizing actual printed bills x 12: six AG-C meters at $43.00/mo = $516.00/yr each (sids 4699142630, 4699664198/P072, 4692424863/PUMP#8, 4697631144/'BATH FARMS IRR 100HP K-87', 4692166716/P075, 4699664728/P077) = 6 x 516.00 = $3,096.00; two AG-B meters at $27.47/mo = $329.64/yr each (4699664561/P057, 4698006011) = $659.28; five AG-A1/A2 meters at $20.67/mo = $248.04/yr each (4699664321/P048, 4699664286/P063, 4699664538/PUMP#56, 4699664965/P043, 4699664955/P045) = $1,240.20. Total = 3,096.00 + 659.28 + 1,240.20 = $4,995.48/yr. This figure ASSUMES the idle cycle is representative of a full 12 months; if these are only seasonally idle (e.g. 6 winter months off), the realizable standby saving is roughly half. The whole-account trulyIdleNonNem latest cycle was $436.96, which x 12 = $5,243.52 across all 14 truly-idle meters and reconciles with this finding plus the P069 dead meter above ($4,995.48 + $248.04 = $5,243.52).
findings.recommendation: SUSPEND, do not remove. These pumps are healthy (GOOD) and are idle only because they are not irrigating this cycle, so the meter must stay re-energizable. First pull trailing-12-month interval data to confirm whether each is idle year-round (true standby candidate) or only off-season; only the genuinely year-round-idle pumps justify a service suspension. For year-round-idle GOOD pumps, ask PG&E for a temporary service disconnect / seasonal turn-off so the daily customer charge stops while the service point is preserved for reconnection (no new-service line-extension cost). The six AG-C idle pumps are the highest-value subset ($516/yr each) and ALSO overlap the rate-arbitrage lens: even if kept energized, an idle AG-C meter belongs on AG-A1 ($20.95 vs $43.58/mo customer charge, saving ~$272/yr/meter and eliminating all demand exposure). Do not double-count: a meter that is suspended saves the whole charge; a meter merely re-rated to AG-A1 saves only the customer-charge delta. CAVEAT: PG&E AG service has a 12-month minimum-term stickiness, and seasonal disconnect/reconnect cycles can themselves carry reconnection fees, so this is best for pumps idle for long, contiguous stretches, not ones that cycle on and off monthly.

findings.title: Off-account idle/dead meters across the other 56 PG&E accounts -> opportunity confirmed but un-priced without their bills
findings.evidence: The billing file covers only account 4699664587-8 (45 of the 183 meters). The remaining 138 meters span 56 other PG&E accounts whose bills were not provided, so no standby dollar can be computed for them here. The inventory does flag dead pumps among them: three BAD/OLD meters live outside the billed account -> P059/sid 2707181864 (AGC, OLD, ELKHORN SHOP, acct 2700884486-5), P142/sid 9495121765 (HAGC, BAD, BIG BLOCK, acct 9493367026-0), and P114/sid 8923940171 (HAGFB, BAD, AIRPORT, acct 8922820273-8). At their schedules these would each bleed roughly: AG-C/HAGC ~ $43.58/mo = ~$523/yr, and HAGFB (AG-B class) ~ $27.84/mo = ~$334/yr, so a rough un-confirmed off-account dead-meter standby exposure is on the order of 523 + 523 + 334 = ~$1,380/yr, NOT counted in the headline numbers because the actual bills are missing. Separately, 26 meters carry status NEW WELL (all on off-billing accounts); newly energized wells legitimately show near-zero use during commissioning and are NOT standby waste, so they are excluded.
findings.recommendation: Pull the bills (or 12-month interval/billing-determinant data) for the other 56 accounts and re-run this idle-standby scan, prioritizing the three already-flagged dead meters (P059 OLD, P142 BAD, P114 BAD) as removal candidates and any zero-use GOOD pumps as suspend candidates. Do not treat NEW WELL meters as standby waste. needsBill / needsIntervalData on all off-account meters before any dollar claim.
